elements.sent-2411-001: Continually evaluating large generative models provides a unique challenge.
elements.sent-2411-002: Often, human annotations are necessary to evaluate high-level properties of these models (e.g. in text or images).
elements.sent-2411-003: However, collecting human annotations of samples can be resource intensive, and using other machine learning systems to provide the annotations, or automatic evaluation, can introduce systematic errors into the evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-004: The Prediction Powered Inference (PPI) framework provides a way of leveraging both the statistical power of automatic evaluation and a small pool of labelled data to produce a low-variance, unbiased estimate of the quantity being evaluated for.
elements.sent-2411-005: However, most work on PPI considers a relatively sizable set of labelled samples, which is not always practical to obtain.
elements.sent-2411-006: To this end, we present two new PPI-based techniques that leverage robust regressors to produce even lower variance estimators in the few-label regime.
elements.sent-2411-007: The deployment of machine learning (ML) systems into several areas of modern life has the potential to lead to several positive outcomes, such as faster and more precise medical care \citep{rajpurkar2017chexnet , improved educational tools \citep{jaiswal2021potential, lee2024rise , and a wide variety of professional assistive technologies \citep{dehaerne2022code .}}}
elements.sent-2411-008: However, the ever increasing integration of these systems has resulted in a more drastic need for effective evaluation techniques for determining when these systems suffer from systematic errors \citep{degrave2021ai .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Accurate evaluation in the era of large language models (LLMs) presents a scaling challenge.
elements.sent-2411-010: Traditional evaluation schemes involve collecting several annotated samples from human labellers, and with ML systems quickly changing \citep{tu2023chatlog , the repeated collection of this data can quickly exhaust time and resources.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Recent advances in the development of LLMs have resulted in the widespread availability of reasonably strong predictive models for arbitrary tasks.
elements.sent-2411-012: The proliferation of these models has made the practice of replacing human-annotated labels with the outputs of an LLM for the sake of evaluation, or automatic evaluation, more feasible.
elements.sent-2411-013: Inherent bias in these LLM predictions leads to potentially inaccurate evaluations, even in the case where many examples are available \citep{angelopoulos2023prediction .}
elements.sent-2411-014: In response to this, frameworks such as Prediction Powered Inference (PPI) \citep{angelopoulos2023prediction offer a way to remove the statistical bias of these predictions with a small pool of labelled data.}
elements.sent-2411-015: While previous works studying the PPI framework have focussed on circumstances where 50 or more (usually 200+) labelled examples are available \citep{boyeau2024autoeval, angelopoulos2023ppi++,zrnic2024cross,zrnic2024active , the applicability of PPI in the few-label regime has not been thoroughly investigated --- in this work, we find that empirically, PPI performs poorly (sometimes worse than classical inference) when very few labelled examples are available.}
elements.sent-2411-016: We argue this is an important case, as many developers of ML systems will rarely have access to a large evaluation set for every criteria they wish to evaluate their system for.
elements.sent-2411-017: This is especially true when developing generative models, where important evaluations are often highly qualitative and potentially time-consuming --- a stark contrast from the development of discriminative models, where large evaluation sets and easily automated evaluation metrics tend to be standard.
elements.sent-2411-018: Rather, developers are more likely to use a small pool of hand-labelled examples as a way to guide design decisions in the early stages of developing a generative model.
elements.sent-2411-019: With this in mind, ensuring evaluation with few labels is as efficient and accurate as possible is important for the development of reliable ML systems.
elements.sent-2411-020: Furthermore, the PPI framework is a natural candidate for generative model evaluation, as the large set of unlabelled data PPI requires can be directly generated by the model itself.
elements.sent-2411-021: In this paper, we work towards the goal of improving few-label auto-evaluation by proposing modifications to the PPI framework which can produce lower variance estimates in the low-label regime.
elements.sent-2411-022: Specifically, we do this through emphasizing and re-interpreting the critical role of the tuning parameter $\lambda$.. Our contributions are:.
elements.sent-2411-023: Providing a theoretical analysis of the PPI++ method in the context of mean estimation, and how it relates to univariate regression.
elements.sent-2411-024: Positing two new extensions for PPI inspired by univariate regression techniques that improve upon the base method.
elements.sent-2411-025: Applying these techniques to the task of feature generation rate estimation in the evaluation of an LLM.
elements.sent-2411-026: In this section, we provide details on the estimation problems we approach, their associated data generative process, and its relationship with contemporary automatic evaluation schemes.
elements.sent-2411-027: We are interested in the properties of a distribution of outputs $X$ generated from some model P, $X \sim P$.
elements.sent-2411-028: These outputs can consist of any modality (e.g. text, images, video) and the model can take any form.
elements.sent-2411-029: Consider the binary function $h(X) \in \{0,1\}$ that outputs 1 if $X$ presents a certain feature of interest, and 0 if X does not present that feature.
elements.sent-2411-030: This notion can be extended to non-binary functions, but this work will focus on the binary case.
elements.sent-2411-031: We wish to estimate the quantity: \begin{equation}\mu_{h,P} := \mathbb{E}_P[h(X)].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-032: We refer to the most well-known estimator for this quantity as the classical estimator, which is simply the sample mean from a finite sample of $n$ example, label pairs $\mathcal{D _n = \{(X_i, h(X_i))\}_{i=1 ^n$.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: This estimate is unbiased, and its variance will decay in order $\frac{1 {n}$.}
elements.sent-2411-034: However, in the regime where labels are scarce or expensive to procure the variance on the sample mean will be high, leading to low-fidelity estimates of the mean.
elements.subsection-2411-002: Prediction Powered Inference for Mean Estimation
elements.sent-2411-035: We next examine how a strong predictive model could be used to complete this estimation task.
elements.sent-2411-036: In our set up, we can consider another binary function, $f(X) \in \{0,1\}$ that is meant to approximate $h(X)$.
elements.sent-2411-037: We can estimate $\mu_{h,P}$ by instead taking the sample mean of a finite pseudolabelled sample $\mathcal{D _N = \{(X^u_i, f(X^u_i))\}_{i=1 ^N$.}}
elements.sent-2411-038: Seeing as both $X$ and $f(X)$ can be generated without human intervention, one can efficiently reach a value of $N$ where the variance of the sample mean is minimized.
elements.sent-2411-039: However, $f(X)$ may be biased.. More rigorously: \begin{equation}\mu_{f,P} := \mathbb{E}_P[f(X)],\quad |\mu_{f,P} - \mu_{h,P}| > 0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-040: If this is true, regardless of how large our sample of $N$ examples is, estimating the mean using pseudolabels will have an irreducible amount of error.
elements.sent-2411-041: In response to the shortcomings of this automatic evaluation approach, \citet{angelopoulos2023prediction leverage a small pool of in-distribution labelled examples along with a larger sample of pseudolabelled examples to create a low variance unbiased estimator in the Prediction Powered Inference (PPI) framework.}
elements.sent-2411-042: The PPI estimate takes as input both the labelled dataset $\mathcal{D _n$ and the pseudolabelled dataset $\mathcal{D _N$, as well as a tuning parameter $\lambda \in \mathds{R}$, and estimates $\mu_{h,P}$ as: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:ppi_estimate} \hat{\mu}_{PPI} := \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N \lambda f(X^u_i) + \frac{1}{n}(\sum_{i=1}^n h(X_i) - \lambda f(X_i)).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-043: This approach, as well as the methods that have succeeded it \citep{angelopoulos2023ppi++, zrnic2024active, boyeau2024autoeval , makes use of both the strong statistical power that traditional automatic evaluation schemes promise, as well as the asymptotic benefits of being unbiased provided by traditional evaluation.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Since its inception, the PPI framework has been the subject of much investigation, both in terms of application and improving the method.
elements.sent-2411-045: While these works focus on how to apply the PPI framework under different assumptions, we instead focus on improving the efficacy of the method in its original setting.
elements.sent-2411-046: We build upon this work by investigating new use cases and improving performance in the low-label regime.
elements.sent-2411-047: Several techniques for supplementing a model's training data with synthetic data have been proposed.
elements.sent-2411-048: This includes both data augmentation techniques such as Mixup \citep{zhang2017mixup and those used in reinforcement learning \citep{pitis2022mocoda, osinski2020simulation .}}
elements.sent-2411-049: Improved generative models have provided a new avenue for researchers and practitioners alike to create new datasets for both training and evaluation \citep{neuhausen2020using .}
elements.sent-2411-050: We contribute to this branch of research by building upon a method for evaluating a generative model with synthetic data generated by the model itself.
elements.sent-2411-051: The use of an additional, correlated variable as a way to reduce the variance of an estimator is a well studied technique in the fields of statistics and machine learning.
elements.sent-2411-052: Previous works have specifically investigated how one can reduce the variance in mean estimation using a control variate constructed with unlabelled data in a regime known as semi-supervised learning \citep{zhang2019semi, zhang2022high .}
elements.sent-2411-053: Furthermore, the use of different regression methods and control variates has also been explored \citep{south2023regularized, blanchet2024can .}
elements.sent-2411-054: In this work, we use this existing literature to better understand and improve the PPI++ method.
elements.sent-2411-055: In this section, we discuss our approach to improving the PPI estimator in the few-label regime.
elements.sent-2411-056: Our approach prioritizes reducing variance and focuses on the role of $\lambda$ in PPI, re-interpreting the task of choosing $\lambda$ as post-hoc regression.
elements.sent-2411-057: We first draw the connection between optimizing $\lambda$ and linear regression, and then discuss how choosing $\lambda$ correctly can be challenging in the low-label regime, resulting in high variance estimates.
elements.sent-2411-058: We then highlight two tools from the regression literature which can be used to improve these estimates, and demonstrate how they fit into the PPI framework.
elements.sent-2411-059: To understand how selecting $\lambda$ appropriately can reduce the variance of $\hat{\mu _{PPI}$, we first provide an expression for the variance of $\hat{\mu _{PPI}$, and decompose it into $Var[\mathcal{D _n]$ and $Var[\mathcal{D _N]$: \begin{equation}Var[\hat{\mu}_{PPI}] = \underbrace{Var[\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N \lambda f(X^u_i)]}_{Var[\mathcal{D}_N]} + \underbrace{Var[\frac{1}{n}(\sum_{i=1}^n h(X_i) - \lambda f(X_i))]}_{Var[\mathcal{D}_n]}.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-060: Note that we are able to distribute the variance operator this way as the two samples are i.i.d.. .
elements.sent-2411-061: Given that we assume that $n << N$ (e.g. we can generate a large amount of unlabeled data cheaply), $Var[\mathcal{D _N]$ will be negligible and we may turn our focus to $Var[\mathcal{D _n]$.}}
elements.sent-2411-062: We can further decompose this variance as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:var_reduction} \begin{aligned} Var[\mathcal{D}_n] = \frac{1}{n^2}\sum_{i=1}^n Var[ h(X_i) - \lambda f(X_i)] = \frac{1}{n^2}\sum_{i=1}^n Var[ h(X) ] + \lambda^2 Var[ f(X)] - 2 \lambda Cov[h(X), f(X)]. \end{aligned}\end{equation} The first term in this sum is equal to the variance of the classical estimate (the labelled sample mean) for $\mu_{h,P}$.

elements.sent-2411-063: Therefore, $\hat{\mu _{PPI}$ will have lower variance than the sample mean estimate whenever $\lambda^2 Var[f(X)] - 2 \lambda Cov[h(X), f(X)] < 0$.}

However, we suggest that $\lambda$ may more accurately be interpreted as a post-hoc transformation of $f$ to make it ``closer to'' $h$; that is, minimizing PPI variance through equivalently minimizing the mean-squared error of the post-hoc regression problem, which has the same form as Equation \ref{eq:var_reduction : \begin{equation}\label{eq:mean_squared_error} \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n(h(X_i) - \lambda f(X_i) - b)^2 \approx \hat{Var}[h(X)] + \hat{Var}[\lambda f(X)] - 2\hat{Cov}[h(X), \lambda f(X)].\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-073: We note that this interpretation better aligns with the fact that PPI is still valid for $\lambda$ outside the $[0, 1]$ interval, and indeed in some situations may achieve its minimal variance at one of these values of $\lambda$\footnote{\citet{angelopoulos2023ppi++ note explicitly that in the mean estimation case (unlike more general cases), $\lambda$ need not be clipped at [0, 1].. .}}
elements.sent-2411-074: Thinking of the $\lambda$-selection problem simply as univariate regression allows for new intuitions around the challenges of few-label PPI.
elements.sent-2411-075: It is known that ordinary least squares (OLS) is an unbiased estimator, but can suffer from high variance when few examples are available.
elements.sent-2411-076: In regression, a standard way to overcome issues with variance is to use ridge regression.
elements.sent-2411-077: This estimator imposes an L2 penalty in addition to the MSE to reduce the variance of the estimate (at the price of adding a small amount of bias).
elements.sent-2411-078: We propose using ridge regression to robustly estimate $\lambda$ in the case where $n$ is small.

elements.sent-2411-130: In this work, we expanded on the PPI framework to perform mean estimation when very few labelled examples are available.
elements.sent-2411-131: By relating the optimal $\lambda$ setting for PPI++ in mean estimation to the regression coefficient, we provide theoretical motivation for two regression-inspired approaches to mean estimation.
elements.sent-2411-132: Both of these approaches use insights from univariate regression to reduce the variance of the estimate when few labels are available.
elements.sent-2411-133: Through experiments estimating the refusal rate of different LLMs, we demonstrate that our approaches produce lower variance estimates than both classical estimation as well as PPI in the low-label regime.
elements.sent-2411-134: Through experiments on subsets of our data, we further elaborate on when our methods can have the most impact.
elements.sent-2411-135: The use of predictive models to aid statistical inference is an exciting research frontier.
elements.sent-2411-136: Future work should investigate efficient methods for performing PPI when the labelled and unlabelled samples come from different distributions.
elements.sent-2411-137: Furthermore, the potential impact of the predictive model having varying levels of performance on different subgroups of the distribution and the potential fairness related concerns that come with it is an important future research direction.. \bibliographystyle{icml2024 .}

elements.sent-2411-244: Through experiments estimating feature generation rates in the context of data analysis and LLM evaluation, we demonstrate that our approaches produce lower variance estimates than both classical estimation as well as PPI in the low-label regime.
elements.sent-2411-245: Through additional analyses and experiments, we further elaborate on when our methods can have the most impact.
elements.sent-2411-246: Furthermore, the potential impact of the predictive model having varying levels of performance on different subgroups of the distribution and the potential fairness related concerns that come with it is an important future research direction.
elements.sent-2411-247: Our work investigates the shortcomings of the Prediction Powered Inference framework and proposes new methods to help overcome these problems.
elements.sent-2411-248: PPI has been applied to several fields of great impact to society, such as ecological, social science, and biological research, as well as the evaluation of widely used machine learning systems like LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-249: These improvements to PPI have the potential to lead to expedited progress in these application areas by reducing errors in statistical inference.
elements.sent-2411-250: However, while we may have uncovered some cases where PPI does not perform as expected, this is certainly not to be exhaustive.

elements.sent-2411-254: In this section, we work through the complete decomposition of the variance of PPI assuming an stochastic but independent estimator for $\lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-255: We analyze the expected mean squared error of PPI++: \begin{equation}\mathbb{E}[(\mu_{h_P} -(\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N \lambda f(X^u_i) + \frac{1}{n}(\sum_{i=1}^n h(X_i) - \lambda f(X_i))))^2] = \mathbb{E}[(\mu_{h_P} -(a + \lambda b - \lambda c))^2]\end{equation} To simplify expressions, we take the following shorthands: \begin{equation}a = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n h(X_i), \quad b = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N f(X^u_i), \quad c = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n f(X_i)\end{equation} We know that the risk of an MSE loss can be written as a bias-variance decomposition.
